note:
新的泄露文件显示了路透社和英国广播公司的参与秘密英国FCO计划，以实现“态度变化”，并“削弱俄罗斯国家的影响力”，与英特尔承包商和Bellingcat一起。
  英国外交部和英联邦办事处（FCO）赞助了路透社和BBC，进行了一系列旨在促进俄罗斯内部变革的秘密方案，并根据一系列泄露的文件，在东欧和中亚破坏其政府。
 泄露的材料显示汤森路透基金会和BBC媒体行动参与旨在反击俄罗斯的封面信息战活动。在英国FCO内通过被称为柜台消号＆amp的阴影部门工作;媒体开发（CDMD），媒体组织在一个简单地称为“财团”的秘密实体中与智力承包商的集合一起运营。
 通过俄罗斯记者培训方案监督路透社，英国外国办事处寻求在“参与者的态度变化”中，促进他们“对英国的感知”的“积极影响”。
 “这些启示展示，当MPS有关俄罗斯的栏杆时，英国代理商正在使用BBC和路透社恰恰是政治家和媒体评论人指责俄罗斯的同一策略，”克里斯威廉姆斯试图申请的英国劳工议员公众对CDMD的隐秘活动进行了审查，并在国家安全场地上是Stonewally，告诉Grayzone。
 “英国广播公司和路透社将自己描绘成一个无法灵活的，公正和权威的世界新闻来源，”威廉姆森继续，“但两者现在都受到这些披露的危害。像这样的双重标准只是让建立政治家和企业媒体黑客进一步蒙羞。“
 Thomson Reuters Foundation Spokesperson Jenny Vereker隐含地确认了泄露文件的真实性，以电子邮件回复了Grayzone的问题。然而，她争辩，“Thomson Reuters基金会从事”秘密活动“的推断是不准确和歪曲我们对公共利益的工作。我们几十年来公开支持一个自由媒体，并致力于在全球协助记者开发出于独立报告所需的技能。“ 
通过黑客集体呼叫本身匿名，泄露文件的泄露文件与2018年和2020年间发布的相关文件密切相关。同一个来源声称获得最新一轮文件的信贷。
 Grayzone于10月20日报告的燕匿本次泄露的材料，该材料暴露于英国FCO资助的大规模宣传活动，以培养对叙利亚政权变化的支持。不久之后，外国办事处声称其计算机系统已经被黑客渗透，从而确认了他们的真实性。
 新的泄漏表明了令人惊叹的细节，路透社和英国广播公司 - 世界上最大和最杰出的新闻组织中的两个 - 试图回答英国外交部的呼吁，帮助改善其“回应和促进俄罗斯的信息的能力” ，“并”抵制俄罗斯政府的叙述“。据CDMD董事称，英国FCO的指定目标是“削弱俄罗斯国家对其附近邻居的影响。”
 路透社和BBC招揽的百万美元合同推进英国国家的干预措施，希望通过FCO资助的旅游和培训课程培养俄罗斯记者，在俄罗斯及其周围建立影响网络，促进俄语地区的亲北约叙述。
 在英国外国办事处的几个建议中，路透社私自一家全球影响力，包括15,000名记者和工作人员，其中包括400个俄罗斯。
 英国FCO项目暗示，据称与众所周知，兼伙伴关系，高调的在线媒体服装，包括Bellingcat，Meduza和Pussy Riot-Gounded Mediazona。 Bellingcat的参与显然包括代表Pro-Nato候选人的北马其顿2019年选举中英国FCO干预。
 监督该行动的情报承包商，锌网络，吹嘘“在俄罗斯和中亚的YouTubers网络”，同时“支持参与者[to]，并在未被登记为外部资金来源的情况下获得国际款项。”该公司还推出了“激活一系列内容”，以支持俄罗斯内部的反政府抗议。 
新文件提供了北约成员国像英国的角色影响到2020年白俄罗斯的颜色革命式抗议活动的关键背景，并提高了关于周围的阴谋和骚乱的令人不安的俄罗斯反对人物阿列克谢·纳瓦尼的问题。
 此外，该材料对世界上两名最大和最负盛名的媒体组织的独立性来说，揭示路透社和英国广播公司的独立性令人疑问，这是一个在英国国家安全国家的低谷上盛宴的表观情报削减，他们的新闻业务越来越厌恶仔细审查。
  一系列官方文件于2020年1月解除了官方文件，揭示了路透社，在20世纪60年代和20世纪70年代，英国政府秘密资助了MI6智能局的反苏联宣传组织。英国政府将英国广播公司担任通行证，以隐瞒向新闻集团的付款。
 启示揭示促使路透社发言人宣布“1969年的安排[与MI6]不与我们的信任原则保持一致，我们今天不会这样做。”
 信托原则概述了“保留[路透社”独立，完整性和自由的使命，从收集和传播信息和新闻中的偏见。“
 在自己的价值表中，BBC宣布，“信任是BBC的基础。我们是独立，公正和诚实的。“
 然而，Grayzone分析的新泄露文件似乎透露，路透社和BBC都与英国外交部抵消和破坏俄罗斯的非透明关系再次参与。 
2017年，路透社媒体帝国的非营利组织汤姆森路透基金会（TRF）进行了正式招标，以“与英国驻莫斯科大使馆代表的外交大臣签订合同，提供“俄罗斯媒体的能力建设”项目。这封信是由路透社首席执行官莫妮克·维勒于2017年7月31日签署的。
  路透社的招标是对FCO招标的回应，FCO寻求帮助实施“俄罗斯记者和在线有影响力人士前往英国的主题旅行计划”。
 FCO通过英国驻莫斯科大使馆的工作，力求在参与者中产生“态度上的变化”，以促进对其“对英国的认知”的“积极影响”。
  FCO在2019年提出了一项类似的倡议，这次阐明了一项更加激进的计划，以“应对俄罗斯政府对媒体和信息空间的叙述和统治。”实际上，英国政府正试图渗透俄罗斯媒体，并通过在英国受过训练的俄罗斯记者的影响力网络传播自己的叙述。
  路透社对FCO的两个电话都进行了详细的招标。在第一次竞标中，这家媒体巨头吹嘘通过“能力建设干预措施”建立一个由15,000名记者和博主组成的全球网络。在俄罗斯，它声称通过其培训计划培养了至少400名记者。
  路透社声称曾代表英国驻莫斯科大使馆对80名俄罗斯记者进行过10次培训。它提出了另外八项建议，承诺促进“英国的文化和政治价值观”，并“在整个俄罗斯建立新闻工作者网络”，这由“对英国事务的共同兴趣”联系在一起。
  路透社的招标书突出了强调培训计划的机构偏见和干预主义议程。路透社详细介绍了一系列由英国FCO资助的旨在“反击俄罗斯国家资助的宣传”的计划，将俄罗斯政府的言论与极端主义混为一谈。具有讽刺意味的是，它把自己削弱它们的努力称为“公正的新闻”。 
与此同时，路透社似乎认识到与英国大使馆在莫斯科的秘密合作是高度挑衅性的，潜在地对外交关系造成的。在英国政府被告莫斯科毒害俄罗斯·俄罗斯智力官员中，叙述了英国的俄罗斯记者，为俄罗斯记者举行了俄罗斯记者，罗吉·俄罗斯·俄罗斯官员闻到英国的俄罗斯人，“[汤姆森路透基金会]与莫斯科大使馆不断沟通，评估风险水平，包括对大使馆的声誉风险。“
  通过白俄罗斯电视台Belsat的路透社提到，其特殊的相关性“对英国政府战略检测和抵消俄罗斯信息传播”是值得注意的。在将自己描述为“白俄罗斯的第一个独立电视频道”，“Belsat是，因为路透社招标明确，北约的载体。
 基于波兰并由波兰外交部和其他欧盟政府提供资金，Belsat在推动2020年5月爆发的颜色革命式抗议方面发挥了影响力，以要求白俄罗斯总统亚历山大卢卡努科的欧斯特。
 最终，路透社的出价似乎已经成功，因为它收到了2019年7月与FCO的冲突，稳定与amp合同;安全基金（CSSF）。但没有实体似乎希望公众了解他们在旨在对抗俄罗斯的项目的合作。合同标志着“严格保密”。
   通过最新的文件泄露的计划在外交和英联邦制定办事处的阴影部门的主持下运作，称为COUNDER DISINATION＆amp;媒体发展（CDMD）。该计划由一个名为Andy Pryce的智力手术，笼罩着秘密。
 事实上，英国政府否认了克里斯威廉姆森等议会关于该司的预算和石砌成员的信息申请自由，他们寻求有关其预算和议程，引用国家安全，阻止他们的信息需求。
 “当我尝试进一步探讨时，”前议员威廉姆森告诉Grayzone，“部长们拒绝让我可以访问与本组织的活动有关的任何文件或通信。我被告知，释放这些信息可能会“破坏和破坏计划的有效性”。 
在2018年6月26日在伦敦召开的会议期间，Pryce概述了一个新的FCO计划“削弱了俄罗斯国家对其附近邻居的影响。”他招揽了一家公司，协助英国国家建立新的和看似独立的媒体网点，在莫斯科的立即影响俄罗斯政府支持的媒体，并扩大北约一对准政府的消息传递。
 在俄罗斯的基础上，俄罗斯认为有意“播种与民主程序的播种和课程”中断，“竞选普罗斯规定更具侵略性，而不是俄罗斯在西方被捕获的任何东西。
  Pryce强调，保密是本质的，警告“一些受助人”不希望与FCO相关联。“
  一年后，FCO的CDMD部门概述了一个计划经营2022年，费用为830万美元到英国纳税人。它旨在建立新的网点并支持预先存在的媒体行动“以反击俄罗斯播种性的努力”和“增加波罗的海国家敌对克里姆林宫的恢复力”。“
 因此，英国政府与一系列情报承包商一起占据了波罗的海媒体，通过亲北约消息传递 - 也许播种了自己的一些差异。
  如下所示，BBC放置了一个明显成功的竞标，通过其非营利性臂参与隐蔽的波罗的海计划，称为BBC媒体行动。
  英国广播公司还建议参加乌克兰，摩尔多瓦和格鲁吉亚的单独英国FCO媒体宣传计划。它命名为传道者和一个叫做Aktis战略的反路障，参与以前的FCO CDMD计划，作为其联盟的关键盟友。 
英国广播公司确定当地的合作伙伴像Hromadske，基于基辅广播网诞生于2014年所谓的迈丹“尊严的革命”之中，关于极端民族主义的肌肉依赖于删除当选总统，并安装一个亲北约政权。 Hromadske几乎在一夜之间有了种子资金，并获得了美国国际开发署（USAID）和亿万富翁媒体大亨Pierre Omidyar的网络基金的后勤支持。
  BBC Media Action提议通过Aktis合作，在乌克兰东部的Donbas地区等冲突地区培养和发展支持北约的媒体，自2014年以来，西方支持的乌克兰军方与支持俄罗斯的分离主义分子之间爆发了一场代理战争。这是教科书信息战，使广播媒体有能力在一场旷日持久的激烈冲突中扭转战局。
  英国FCO宣传运动警告说，“克里姆林宫附属建筑物”如果暴露在外，可能会破坏该项目。对于一家声称将信任作为其价值宪章的核心的媒体组织而言，英国广播公司无疑是高度保密的。
  英国FCO介入东欧和波罗的海，在寻求在俄罗斯外围提供“能力建设”和媒体发展援助的承包商中引起了极大的轰动。竞标者中有路透社和资深FCO承包商，他们参加了从叙利亚到英国家庭阵线的一系列信息战。
  竞标参加由英国FCO资助的财团的情报承包商包括Zinc Network和Albany Communications。正如记者基特·克拉伦伯格（Kit Klarenberg）在2月18日有关FCO近期泄漏的报告中指出的那样，这些公司“吹嘘拥有[安全]通关的员工，这些人员以前曾在政府，军事和安全部门担任最高职务。他们还具有代表伦敦代表世界各地开展信息战的丰富经验。”
 Zinc以前称为Breakthrough，已与英国内政部签约，在“防止激进主义”倡议的主持下秘密实施宣传英国穆斯林的媒体项目。在澳大利亚，锌被发现实施一项秘密计划，以促进穆斯林对政府政策的支持。
 本·诺顿（Ben Norton）为《灰色地带》（The Grayzone）报导了奥尔巴尼（“奥尔巴尼”）的记录，“确保由55个以上的纵梁，记者和摄像师组成的广泛本地网络的参与”，以在进行公共关系服务的同时影响媒体报道并推动西方叙利亚政权更替目标的实现。代表由北约成员国和海湾君主制资助的破坏叙利亚稳定的极端主义叙利亚民兵。 
在竞标于波罗的海地区的英国FCO媒体计划中，奥尔巴尼提出了一系列讽刺的“互动游戏”，如“普京宾果”，以鼓励对俄罗斯政府的反对和欧盟俄罗斯人经历的挫折。“
 奥尔巴尼倾向于一个名为Meduza的拉脱维亚的出口，作为“这些游戏的领先支持者”。俄罗斯反对派支持者中的顶级网站，Meduza收到了瑞典政府的财政支持，并获得了几亿烷支持的亲北约基金会。
  作为英国FCO承包商，锌网络表示，它不仅是Meduza的“提供观众细分和针对支持”，而且是Mediazona，这是由反克里姆林宫绩效艺术集团猫骚乱的两名成员创立的一个据称独立的媒体企业。
 Mediazona的创始人之一Nadya Tolokonnikova，在克林顿基金会的2015年会议上分享了与前美国总统比尔克林顿的舞台。次年，Tolokonnikova现在被监禁的Wikileaks创始人Julian Assange，声称，“他与俄罗斯政府有关，我觉得他为此感到骄傲。”
   除了为“独立”推出右线的“独立”插座提供“定位支持”，ZINC提出利用英国FCO资金进入直接支付和游戏谷歌搜索方案的支持。智能剪裁是明确的，这是为了减少俄罗斯政府支持的广播RT.COM的搜索可见性的愿望。
  英国隐瞒并管理了俄罗斯YouTubers的网络和“激活”反政府抗议内容
 在一个标记为“私人和保密”的文件中，ZINC揭示了联盟在俄罗斯和中亚建立“YouTuber网络”的作用，旨在传播英国及其北约盟友的信息。 
据Zinc称，该财团“在不注册为外部资金来源的情况下支持参与者进行国际收支”，大概是为了规避俄罗斯对外资媒体机构的注册要求。
 Zinc还帮助YouTube影响者“制定了编辑策略来传达重要信息”，同时努力“对其参与进行保密”。它以“促进媒体诚信和民主价值观”的名义执行了整个秘密宣传计划。
  俄罗斯YouTube上最杰出的影响者也许是前边缘民族主义反对派人物阿列克谢·纳瓦尼（Alexei Navalny），此人成为引起高调中毒事件的目标后被提名诺贝尔奖，中毒事件使俄罗斯和西方之间的关系达到了冷战后的最低点。
 俄罗斯政府因逃避假释而对纳瓦尼判处2.5年监禁，这激发了新一波的反政府抗议活动。早在2018年，Navalny亲自共同发起了全国示威活动，以禁止加密消息应用程序Telegram的使用。
 Zinc在竞标英国FCO合同时透露，它扮演了幕后角色，“在最近的电报抗议活动发生后的12小时内激活了一系列内容。”这些活动是否涉及纳瓦尼还是他的直接网络尚不清楚，但锌的私人披露似乎证实了英国情报部门在放大2018年抗议活动中发挥了作用。
  俄罗斯情报部门发布了令人震惊的录像带，显示了纳瓦尼FBK反腐败组织的执行董事弗拉基米尔·阿舒尔科夫（Vladimir Ashurkov）于2013年会见了一名怀疑是英国MI6特工詹姆斯·威廉·托马斯·福特（James William Thomas Ford），他正在英国驻莫斯科大使馆附近工作。在会合期间，可以听到阿舒尔科夫要价10到2000万美元，以产生“相当不同的”政治景象。
  2018年，Ashurkov的名字出现在泄露的文件中，该文件暴露了一个秘密的英国FCO影响力网络，称为“诚信倡议”。正如《灰色地带》报道的那样，“诚信倡议”在一个名为“国家工艺研究所”的智囊团的掩护下运作，该智囊团通过在苏格兰的伪造办公室掩盖了自己的位置。 
由一群军事情报官员经营，秘密宣传集团通过媒体和政治影响者的集群努力升级西部和俄罗斯之间的紧张局势。 在伦敦的反俄罗斯影响者中列出的是Ashurkov。  诚信倡议的军事董事概述了Stark，明确的术语议程。 由于以下泄露的备忘录说明，他们旨在利用媒体，智库和他们的影响网络激发俄罗斯据说诽谤的歇斯底里  ......
note_en:
New leaked documents show Reuters’ and the BBC’s involvement in covert UK FCO programs to effect “attitudinal change” and “weaken the Russian state’s influence,” alongside intel contractors and Bellingcat.
  The UK Foreign and Commonwealth Office (FCO) have sponsored Reuters and the BBC to conduct a series of covert programs aimed at promoting regime change inside Russia and undermining its government across Eastern Europe and Central Asia, according to a series of  leaked documents.
 The leaked materials show the Thomson Reuters Foundation and BBC Media Action participating in a covert information warfare campaign aimed at countering Russia. Working through a shadowy department within the UK FCO known as the Counter Disinformation & Media Development (CDMD), the media organizations operated alongside a collection of intelligence contractors in a secret entity known simply as “the Consortium.”
 Through training programs of Russian journalists overseen by Reuters, the British Foreign Office sought to produce an “attitudinal change in the participants,” promoting a “positive impact” on their “perception of the UK.”
 “These revelations show that when MPs were railing about Russia, British agents were using the BBC and Reuters to deploy precisely the same tactics that politicians and media commentators were accusing Russia of using,” Chris Williamson, a former UK Labour MP who  attempted to apply public scrutiny to the CDMD’s covert activities and was stonewalled on national security grounds, told The Grayzone.
 “The BBC and Reuters portray themselves as an unimpeachable, impartial, and authoritative source of world news,” Williamson continued, “but both are now hugely compromised by these disclosures. Double standards like this just bring establishment politicians and corporate media hacks into further disrepute.”
 Thomson Reuters Foundation spokesperson Jenny Vereker implicitly confirmed the authenticity of the leaked documents in an emailed response to questions from The Grayzone. However, she contended, “The inference that the Thomson Reuters Foundation was engaged in ‘secret activities’ is inaccurate and misrepresents our work in the public interest. We have for decades openly supported a free press and have worked to help journalists globally to develop the skills needed to report with independence.”
 The tranche of leaked files closely resemble UK FCO-related documented released between 2018 and 2020 by a hacking collective calling itself Anonymous. The same source has claimed credit for obtaining the latest round of documents.
 The Grayzone reported in October 2020 on leaked materials released by Anonymous which exposed a massive  propaganda campaign funded by the UK FCO to cultivate support for regime change in Syria. Soon after, the Foreign Office  claimed its computer systems had been penetrated by hackers, thus confirming their authenticity.
 The new leaks illustrate in alarming detail how Reuters and the BBC – two of the largest and most distinguished news organizations in the world – attempted to answer the British foreign ministry’s call for help in improving its “ability to respond and to promote our message across Russia,” and to “counter the Russian government’s narrative.” Among the UK FCO’s stated goals, according to the director of the CDMD, was to “weaken the Russian State’s influence on its near neighbours.”
 Reuters and the BBC solicited multimillion-dollar contracts to advance the British state’s interventionist aims, promising to cultivate Russian journalists through FCO-funded tours and training sessions, establish influence networks in and around Russia, and promote pro-NATO narratives in Russian-speaking regions.
 In several proposals to the British Foreign Office, Reuters boasted of a global influence network of 15,000 journalists and staff, including 400 inside Russia.
 The UK FCO projects were carried out covertly, and in partnership with purportedly independent, high-profile online media outfits including Bellingcat, Meduza, and the Pussy Riot-founded Mediazona. Bellingcat’s participation apparently included a UK FCO intervention in North Macedonia’s 2019 elections on behalf of the pro-NATO candidate.
 The intelligence contractors that oversaw that operation, the Zinc Network, boasted of establishing “a network of YouTubers in Russia and Central Asia” while “supporting participants [to] make and receive international payments without being registered as external sources of funding.” The firm also touted its ability to “activate a range of content” to support anti-government protests inside Russia.
 The new documents provide critical background on the role of NATO member states like the UK in influencing the color revolution-style protests waged in Belarus in 2020, and raise unsettling questions about the intrigue and unrest surrounding jailed Russian opposition figure  Alexei Navalny.
 Further, the materials cast serious doubt on the independence of two of the world’s largest and most prestigious media organizations, revealing Reuters and the BBC as apparent intelligence cut-outs feasting at the trough of a British national security state that their news operations are increasingly averse to scrutinizing.
  A series of  official documents declassified in January 2020 revealed that Reuters was secretly funded by the British government throughout the 1960s and 1970s to assist an anti-Soviet propaganda organization run by the MI6 intelligence agency. The UK government used the BBC as a pass-through to conceal payments to the news group.
 The revelation prompted a Reuters spokesman to declare that “the arrangement in 1969 [with the MI6] was not in keeping with our Trust Principles and we would not do this today.”
 The Trust Principles outline a mission of “preserving [Reuters’] independence, integrity, and freedom from bias in the gathering and dissemination of information and news.”
 In its own statement of values, the BBC  proclaims, “Trust is the foundation of the BBC. We’re independent, impartial and honest.”
 However, the newly leaked documents analyzed by The Grayzone appear to reveal that both Reuters and the BBC are engaged yet again in a non-transparent relationship with the UK’s foreign ministry to counter and undermine Russia.
 In 2017, the non-profit arm of the Reuters media empire, the Thomson Reuters Foundation (TRF), delivered a formal tender offering to “enter into a Contract with the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs, as represented by the British Embassy Moscow, for the provision of a project ‘Capacity Building in Russian Media.'” The letter was signed by Reuters CEO Monique Ville on July 31, 2017.
  Reuters’ tender was a response to a call for bids by the FCO, which sought help in implementing “a programme of themed tours to the UK by Russian journalists and online influencers.”
 Working through the British Embassy in Moscow, the FCO sought to produce an “attitudinal change in the participants,” promoting a “positive impact” on their “perception of the UK.”
  In 2019, the FCO put forward a similar initiative, this time articulating a more aggressive plan to “counter the Russian government’s narrative and domination of the media and information space.” In effect, the British government was seeking to infiltrate Russian media and propagate its own narrative through an influence network of Russian journalists trained in the UK.
  Reuters responded to both calls by the FCO with detailed tenders. In its first bid, the media giant boasted of establishing a global network of 15,000 journalists and bloggers through “capacity building interventions.” In Russia, it claimed at least 400 journalists had been cultivated through its training programs.
  Reuters claimed to have performed 10 previous training tours for 80 Russian journalists on behalf of the British embassy in Moscow. It proposed eight more, promising to promote “UK cultural and political values” and “create a network of journalists across Russia” bonded together by a shared “interest in British affairs.”
  Reuters’ tender highlighted the institutional prejudices and interventionist agenda that underlined its training programs. Detailing a series of UK FCO-funded programs dedicated to “countering Russian state-funded propaganda,” Reuters conflated Russian government narratives with extremism. Ironically, it referred to its own efforts at weakening them as “unbiased journalism.”
  At the same time, Reuters appeared to recognize that its covert collaboration with the British Embassy in Moscow was highly provocative and potentially destructive to diplomatic relations. Recounting a UK FCO-funded tour it ran for Russian journalists in the midst of the Sergei Skripal affair, after the British government accused Moscow of poisoning a turncoat Russian intelligence officer who spied for Britain, the tender stated, “[Thomson Reuters Foundation] was in constant communication with the British Embassy in Moscow, to assess levels of risk, including reputational risk to the embassy.”
  The mention by Reuters of the Belarusian TV Station Belsat, and its particular relevance “to the UK Government Strategy’s capacity to detect and counter the spread of Russian information” was notable. While describing itself as “the first independent television channel in Belarus,” Belsat is, as the Reuters tender makes clear, a vehicle of NATO influence.
 Based in Poland and  funded by the Polish Foreign Ministry and other EU governments, Belsat played an  influential role in promoting the color revolution-style protests that erupted in May 2020 to demand the ouster of Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko.
 Ultimately, Reuters’ bid appears to have been successful, as it received a July 2019 contract with the FCO’s Conflict, Stability & Security Fund (CSSF). But neither entity seemed to want the public to know about their collaboration on a project designed to counter Russia. The contract was marked “Strictly Confidential.”
   The programs exposed through the latest leak of documents operate under the auspices of a shadowy division of the Foreign and Commonwealth Development Office called Counter Disinformation & Media Development (CDMD). Led by an intelligence operative named Andy Pryce, the program has shrouded in secrecy.
 Indeed, the British government has  denied freedom of information requests about the division’s budget and stonewalled members of parliament like Chris Williamson who sought data about its budget and agenda, citing national security to block their demands for information.
 “When I tried to probe further,” former MP Williamson told The Grayzone, “ministers refused to let me have access to any documents or correspondence relating to this organization’s activities.  I was told that releasing this information could ‘disrupt and undermine the program’s effectiveness.’”
 During a meeting convened in London on June 26, 2018, Pryce outlined a new FCO program “to weaken the Russian State’s influence on its near neighbors.” He solicited a consortium of firms to assist the British state in establishing new and seemingly independent media outlets to counter Russian government-backed media in Moscow’s immediate sphere of influence, and to amplify the messaging of NATO-aligned governments.
 Justified on the basis of Russia’s supposed intention to “sow disunity and course[sic] disruption to democratic processes,” the campaign Pryce laid out was more aggressive and far-reaching than anything Russia has been caught doing in the West.
  Pryce emphasized that secrecy was of the essence, warning that “some grantees will not wish to be linked to the FCO.”
  A year later, the FCO’s CDMD division outlined a program to run through 2022 at a cost of $8.3 million to the British taxpayer. It aimed to establish new outlets and support preexisting media operations “to counter Russia’s efforts to sow disunity” and “increase resilience to hostile Kremlin messaging in the Baltic states.”
 Thus the British government set out with an array of intelligence contractors to dominate Baltic media with pro-NATO messaging – and perhaps sow some disunity of its own.
  As seen below, the BBC placed an apparently successful bid to participate in the covert Baltic program through its non-profit arm, known as BBC Media Action.
  The BBC also proposed to participate in a separate UK FCO media propaganda program in Ukraine, Moldova, and Georgia. It named Reuters and a now-defunct intelligence contractor called Aktis Strategy, which participated in previous FCO CDMD programs, as key allies in its consortium.
  The BBC identified local partners like Hromadske, a Kiev-based broadcast network born in the midst of the so-called Maidan “Revolution of Dignity” in 2014 that relied on ultra-nationalist muscle to remove an elected president and install a pro-NATO regime. Hromadske materialized almost overnight with  seed money and logistical support from the  US Agency for International Development (USAID) and  billionaire media mogul Pierre Omidyar’s Network Fund.
  BBC Media Action proposed working through Aktis to cultivate and grow pro-NATO media in conflict areas like the Donbas region of eastern Ukraine, where a proxy war has raged since 2014 between the Western-backed Ukrainian military and pro-Russian separatists. It was textbook information warfare, weaponizing broadcast media to turn the tide of battle in a protracted, grinding conflict.
  The UK FCO propaganda campaign warned that “Kremlin-affiliated structures” could undermine the project if it was exposed. For a media organization that claims to place trust at the heart of its charter of values, the BBC was certainly operating under a high degree of secrecy.
  The UK FCO’s meddling in Eastern Europe and the Baltics created a feeding frenzy among contractors seeking to provide “capacity building” and media development assistance on Russia’s periphery. Among the bidders were Reuters and veteran FCO contractors that had participated in an array of information warfare campaigns from Syria to the British home front.
  Among the intelligence contractors bidding to participate in the UK FCO-funded Consortium were the Zinc Network and Albany Communications. As journalist Kit Klarenberg noted in a February 18  report on the recent FCO leaks, these firms “boast staff possessed of [security] clearances, individuals who previously served at the highest levels of government, the military and security services. They furthermore have extensive experience in conducting information warfare operations on London’s behalf the world over.”
 Previously known as Breakthrough, Zinc has contracted for the UK Home Office to  covertly implement media projects propagandizing British Muslims under the auspices of the Prevent de-radicalization initiative. In Australia, Zinc was  caught running a clandestine program to promote support for government policies among Muslims.
 Ben Norton  reported for The Grayzone on Albany’s record of “secur[ing] the participation of an extensive local  network of over 55 stringers, reporters and videographers” to influence media narratives and advance Western regime-change goals in Syria, while conducting public relations services on behalf of extremist Syrian militias funded by NATO member states and Gulf monarchies to destabilize the country.
 In its bid for the UK FCO media program in the Baltic region, Albany proposed a series of satirical “interactive games” like “Putin Bingo” to encourage opposition to the Russian government and exploit “frustrations experienced by Russians in the EU.”
 Albany pitched a Latvia-based outlet called Meduza as “a leading proponent of these games.” A top website among Russian opposition supporters, Meduza has received financial support  from the Swedish government and several billionaire-backed pro-NATO foundations.
  As a UK FCO contractor, the Zinc Network said it was “delivering audience segmentation and targeting support” not only to Meduza, but also to Mediazona, a supposedly independent media venture  founded by two members of the anti-Kremlin performance art group Pussy Riot.
 One of Mediazona’s founders, Nadya Tolokonnikova,  shared a stage with former US President Bill Clinton at the Clinton Foundation’s 2015 conference. The following year, Tolokonnikova  trashed now-imprisoned Wikileaks founder Julian Assange, claiming, “He’s connected with the Russian government, and I feel that he’s proud of it.”
   Besides delivering “targeting support” for “independent” outlets pushing the right line against the Kremlin, Zinc proposed leveraging UK FCO funding into a program of direct payments and gaming Google search results in their favor. The intelligence cut-out was explicit about its desire to reduce the search visibility of the Russian government-backed broadcaster RT.com.
  The UK covertly funded and managed a network of Russian YouTubers and “activated” anti-government protest content
 In a document marked “private and confidential,” Zinc revealed the Consortium’s role in setting up a “YouTuber network” in Russia and Central Asia designed to propagate the message of the UK and its NATO allies.
 According to Zinc, the Consortium was “supporting participants mak[ing] and receiv[ing] international payments without being registered as external sources of funding,” presumably to circumvent Russian registration requirements for foreign-funded media outfits.
 Zinc also helped the YouTube influencers “develop editorial strategies to deliver key messages” while working “to keep their involvement confidential.” And it carried out its entire program of covert propaganda in the name of “promoting media integrity and democratic values.”
  Perhaps the most prominent Russian YouTube influencer is  Alexei Navalny, a previously marginal nationalist opposition figure who was nominated for a Nobel Prize after becoming the target of a high-profile poisoning incident that brought relations between Russia and the West to its post-Cold War nadir.
 The Russian government’s sentencing of Navalny to a 2.5-year prison term for evading parole has inspired a new wave of anti-government protests. Back in 2018,  Navalny personally co-sponsored national demonstrations against the banning of the encrypted messaging app Telegram.
 In its bid for a UK FCO contract, Zinc revealed that it played a behind-the-scenes role “to activate a range of content within 12 hours of the recent telegram protests.” Whether those activities involved Navalny or his immediate network was unclear, but the private disclosure by Zinc appeared confirm that British intelligence played a role in amplifying the 2018 protests.
  Russian intelligence services have released  sting video footage showing Vladimir Ashurkov, the executive director of Navalny’s FBK anti-corruption organization, meeting in 2013 with a suspected British MI6 agent named James William Thomas Ford, who was operating out of the British embassy in Moscow. During the rendezvous, Ashurkov can be heard asking for 10 to 20 million dollars to generate “quite a different picture” of the political landscape.
  In 2018, Ashurkov’s name appeared in leaked documents exposing a covert, UK FCO influence network called the Integrity Initiative. As  The Grayzone reported, the Integrity Initiative operated behind the cover of a think tank called the Institute for Statecraft, which concealed its own location through a fake office in Scotland.
 Run by a group of military intelligence officers, the secret propaganda group worked through clusters of media and political influencers to escalate tensions between the West and Russia.  Listed among the London cluster of anti-Russian influencers was Ashurkov.
 The Integrity Initiative’s military directors outlined their agenda in stark, unequivocal terms. As the leaked memo below illustrates, they aimed to exploit the media, think tanks and their influence network to stir up as much hysteria about Russia’s supposedly malign

title: 英国广播公司路透社（BBC）参加了由英国外交部资助的秘密活动
title_en: Reuters, BBC, participated in covert UK Foreign Office-funded programs

旧金山的校董会主席面临多个方面的巨大压力，包括学校重新开放以及最近决定停止在洛厄尔高中的择优录取，这表明旧金山的校董会正在暂停有争议的努力，以重命名44所地区学校。
   加布里埃拉·洛佩斯（GabrielaLópez）在周日在SFChronicle.com和周一《纪事报》的印刷版中发表的意见书中承认，该程序中的错误导致了潜在的诉讼，并召回了针对包括洛佩斯在内的数名董事会成员的努力。
  她写道：“在我们努力重新开放学校的过程中，发生了许多分散注意力的公开辩论。” “学校重命名就是其中之一。这是一个始于2018年的过程，其时间表预计不会大流行。我承认并承担重命名过程中所犯的错误。”
  洛佩兹说，重新开放学校的工作将是董事会的“唯一重点”，与此同时取消重命名委员会的会议。她说，董事会计划通过让当地历史学家参与进来，使这一过程更具“审议性”。
     她写道：“与此同时，这是我最后一次公开重命名，直到学校重新开放为止。” “我们将不会在董事会议程上花费宝贵的时间来进一步讨论这一问题，因为我们需要优先考虑重新开放。”
    2月10日，旧金山律师保罗·斯科特（Paul D. Scott）向洛佩斯（López）发送了一封要求信，指称违反了加利福尼亚州的公开会议法《布朗法案》。他辩称，董事会没有适当地通知公众，它正在做出重命名学校地点的最终决定，并仅提前通知它将决定要重命名的“潜在”学校名单。
   他说，拒绝了家庭和公众的正当程序，而应逐个学校处理此事。这封信给了董事会30天的时间来推翻该决定，否则将采取法律行动。周二，董事会召开了长达七个小时的非公开特别会议，大概是解决了法律挑战和重命名问题。 
在回应意见书时，斯科特说，他很高兴董事会“明显地看到了理由”，但是他希望在决定是否继续采取任何法律行动之前“以书面形式提出细节”。
  他说：“这种变化不应强加给我们当地社区。” “与每所学校有联系的父母，学生，教师，校友和其他人都是真正的利益相关者。应该信任他们为自己的学校做出好的判断，并尊重他们的观点。”
  1月27日，校务委员会以6-1投票通过对44所学校进行更名，因为这些学校拥有从Dianne Feinstein小学到Jefferson小学到Abraham Lincoln高中的历史人物名字，据说与种族主义和压迫有关。
  批评重命名的批评者指出，任命委员会的建议是基于与奴隶制，压迫，种族主义和殖民化的联系而对学校进行更名的错误。在某些情况下，委员会成员依靠Wikipedia来决定名称是否符合标准。
  以阿拉莫小学为例，委员会援引了阿拉莫之战及其与德克萨斯革命期间占领墨西哥土地的联系。但实际上，这所学校更可能以西班牙语的“杨木树”一词来命名，该树基于识别该城市旅行者的休息站的树，现在是阿拉莫广场。
   洛厄尔高中校友会执行理事特伦斯·阿巴德（Terence Abad）说：“我很高兴听到校董会对这一程序的缺陷有深刻的了解。” “像大多数人一样，我确实认为重命名过程很重要，我欢迎以有序和适当的方式进行。”
  阿巴德（Abad）指出，由于改名过程，其他研究表明，洛厄尔（Lowell）可能不是以诗人詹姆斯·洛厄尔（James Lowell）的名字命名，而只是以可能基于马萨诸塞州洛厄尔的洛厄尔为名字，并给了该校长1894年提到的一封信改名为“洛厄尔中学（Lowell High School）”。 
该决定引起了全国的关注，并受到领导者和父母的批评​​，因为在大流行期间家庭苦苦挣扎时，时机不佳。 10月份，伦敦市长伦敦市议会抨击了重命名计划。
  同时，学校官员面临着重新开放教室的压力，教室已经关闭了将近一年。在周三的新闻发布会上，洛佩兹和旧金山统一警司文森特·马修斯说，官员们正试图尽快恢复面对面的学习，但拒绝猜测第一批学生何时会回来。该地区最近表示，目标是在6月2日学年结束之前进行为期六周的局部现场教学。
  旧金山的市检察官起诉学区，指称官员们未能按照州法律的要求制定具体的重新开放计划，并且尽管有能力也没有亲自授课，但违反了州宪法和平等权利法。
   这场争议助长了罢免董事会三名成员的努力，其中包括洛佩斯总统，副总统艾莉森·柯林斯和专员法奥加·莫利加。
  这项工作于周五正式启动，并向选举官员提交了文件，三名成员每人都需要70,000个签名才能进行投票召回。五个孩子的父母的组织者Autumn Looijen和Siva Raj说，他们希望“摆脱政治的教育”。
    编者注：这个故事的先前版本错误地指出，居民已签署请愿书以召回学校董事会成员。他们实际上已经签署了召回意向书。
   Kellie Hwang和Jill Tucker是《旧金山纪事报》的撰稿人。电子邮件：kellie.hwang @ sfchronicle.com，jtucker @ sfchronicle.com Twitter：@ KellieHwang，@ jilltucker 
Kellie Hwang是旧金山纪事的参与记者。在返回湾区之前，她在印第安纳波利斯的Indystar举行了作为运输记者和趋势的新闻记者。此前，Kellie涵盖了亚利桑那共和国的餐饮新闻和趋势，视觉艺术，活动和夜生活，为前反对哥斯达时报的自由塑造。 Kellie还担任亚裔美国人记者协会特征论坛的联合主任。她是华盛顿大学毕业生。
 Jill Tucker在加利福尼亚覆盖了22年的教育，写出了湾区学区面临的问题的故事，以更广泛的国家政策辩论。她的工作产生了对州法律的变化，并刺激了政治和社区行动，以解决当地的需求。
 她是kqed＆＃39; s＆＃34的常客。纽室＆＃34;电视节目和＆＃34;论坛＆＃34;广播节目。贝尔地区，吉尔赢得了大师的硕士＆＃39;科罗拉多大学，博尔德和一个学士学位的新闻学位。来自UC Santa Barbara的学士学位。介于两者之间，她花了两年的佛得角，西非佛得角志愿者教学英语。

Facing intense pressure on multiple fronts including  school reopenings and a recent decision to  halt merit-based admissions at Lowell High School, San Francisco’s school board president has signaled that its members are pausing controversial efforts to rename 44 district schools.
   In an  opinion piece published Sunday on SFChronicle.com and in Monday’s print editions of The Chronicle, Gabriela López acknowledged mistakes in the process that have resulted in a  potential lawsuit and  recall efforts targeting several board members, including López.
  “There have been many distracting public debates as we’ve been working to reopen our schools,” she wrote. “School renaming has been one of them. It was a process begun in 2018 with a timeline that didn’t anticipate a pandemic. I acknowledge and take responsibility that mistakes were made in the renaming process.”
  López said working to reopen schools will be the board’s “only focus,” and renaming committee meetings will be canceled in the meantime. She said the board plans to make the process more “deliberative” by getting local historians involved.
     “In the meantime, this is the last time I’ll comment publicly on renaming until schools are reopened,” she wrote. “We will not be taking valuable time from our board agendas to further discuss this, as we need to prioritize reopening.”
    On Feb. 10., San Francisco attorney Paul D. Scott sent a  demand letter addressed to López alleging a violation of the Brown Act, California’s open-meeting law. He argued that the board did not properly notify the public that it was making a final decision to rename the school sites and provided advance notice only that it would decide on a list of “potential” schools to be renamed.
   He said families and the public were denied due process, and the matter instead should be handled on a school-by-school basis. The letter gave the board 30 days to reverse the decision or legal action would be taken. On Tuesday, the board held a seven-hour  closed special meeting that presumably addressed the legal challenge and renaming issue.
  In response to the opinion piece, Scott said he is glad the board has “evidently seen reason,” but he wants “specifics in writing” before deciding whether to continue with any legal action.
  “Changes of this magnitude should not be imposed from on high on our local communities,” he said. “Parents, students, teachers, alumni and others connected to each school are the true stakeholders. They should be trusted to make good judgments for their particular school and have their point of view be respected.”
  On Jan. 27, the school board voted 6-1 to rename 44 schools because they bear the names of historical figures said to be linked to racism and oppression, from Dianne Feinstein Elementary to Jefferson Elementary to Abraham Lincoln High School.
  Critics of the renaming cited errors made by the committee appointed to recommend schools to be renamed based on connections to slavery, oppression, racism and colonization. In several cases, the committee members relied on Wikipedia to make a decision on whether a name met criteria.
  In the case of Alamo Elementary, the committee cited the Battle of the Alamo and its connection to the taking of Mexican land during the Texas Revolution. But in reality, the school was more likely named after the Spanish word for cottonwood tree, based on the tree that identified a traveler’s rest station in the city, which is now Alamo Square.
   “I’m delighted to hear that the school board has an understanding of how flawed the process was,” said Terence Abad, executive director of the alumni association for Lowell High School, which was also on the list for renaming. “Like most people, I do think the process on renaming is important, and I welcome doing it in an orderly and appropriate way.”
  Abad noted that because of the renaming process, additional research has shown that Lowell likely wasn’t named after James Lowell, the poet, but rather just Lowell, perhaps based on Lowell, Mass., given a letter from the district superintendent in 1894 referred to the school’s new name as only “Lowell High School.”
    The decision garnered national attention and  has been criticized by leaders and parents for being poorly timed while families struggle during the pandemic. In October, Mayor London Breed  slammed the renaming plan.
  Meanwhile, pressure has been building on school officials to reopen classrooms, which have been shut for almost a year. At a news conference Wednesday, Lopez and San Francisco Unified Superintendent Vincent Matthews said officials are trying to resume in-person learning as soon as possible, but declined to guess when the first students might be back. The district said recently the goal is to have six weeks of partial live instruction before the school year ends June 2.
  San Francisco’s city attorney has  sued the school district, alleging that officials failed to create a specific reopening plan as required by state law, and violated the state Constitution and equal rights laws by not providing in-person instruction despite the ability to do so.
   The controversy helped fuel an effort to recall three members of the board, including President López, Vice President Alison Collins and Commissioner Faauuga Moliga.
  The effort, officially launched Friday with the submission of papers to election officials, will need 70,000 signatures for each of the three members to get the recall on the ballot. Organizers Autumn Looijen and Siva Raj, who are parents of five children, said they wanted to “get politics out of education.”
    Editor’s note: A previous version of this story incorrectly stated that residents had signed a petition to recall school board members. They had in fact signed an expression of interest in a recall.
   Kellie Hwang and Jill Tucker are San Francisco Chronicle staff writers. Email:  kellie.hwang@sfchronicle.com,  jtucker@sfchronicle.com Twitter:  @KellieHwang,  @jilltucker
  Kellie Hwang is the engagement reporter for The San Francisco Chronicle. Before returning to the Bay Area, she held roles as transportation reporter and trending news reporter at the IndyStar in Indianapolis. Previously, Kellie covered dining news and trends, visual arts, events and nightlife for the Arizona Republic, and freelanced for the former Contra Costa Times. Kellie also serves as co-director of the Asian American Journalists Association Features Forum. She is a University of Washington graduate.
 Jill Tucker has covered education in California for 22 years, writing stories that range from issues facing Bay Area school districts to broader national policy debates. Her work has generated changes to state law and spurred political and community action to address local needs.
 She is a frequent guest on KQED's "Newroom" television show and "Forum" radio show. A Bay Area native, Jill earned a master's degree in journalism at the University of Colorado, Boulder and a bachelor's degree from the UC Santa Barbara. In between, she spent two years as a Peace Corps volunteer teaching English in Cape Verde, West Africa.

 在不熟悉不同类型的闪存存储之间的差异的情况下，Apple使用TOSHIBA TLC NAND Flash在M1 Mac中的驱动器到目前为止。这些被评为每天大约一个完整的驱动器写。
 SSD中的单元在三级单元的闪存芯片中持续大约3,000个读取和写入周期，但对于中端多电平电池的芯片，更通常约为10,000个周期。即便如此，这仍然基于每天一个完整的驱动器写入八年的使用量。

 当驱动器生产商在驱动器中包括额外的存储容量时，这也没有考虑到过度配置的概念。这并不是要给用户更多的容量，更多的是将逐渐磨损的驱动器分散到更多的单元上，从而进一步延长其使用寿命。
 我们最终不知道苹果使用的是什么质量的芯片，也不知道超额配置的水平。但是，随着Apple首次涉足Mac芯片，苹果在提供更好的用户体验和两方面都慷慨方面会犯错似乎是合乎逻辑的。

  在现阶段，没有任何外部迹象表明苹果正计划广泛讨论此事。鉴于有可能在头条新闻中宣布“ New Mac mini将在一年内死亡”。或类似问题，似乎是苹果公司可能希望拦截而不是让其滚雪球般的公关问题。
 这件事还引发了确定Mac运行状况的软件工具问题。尽管Apple在macOS中包含了电池运行状况管理元素，但对于驱动器的运行状况却不能这么说。 
macOS为此目的最接近的工具是“磁盘工具”，但是尽管它对管理磁盘很有用，但在确定驱动器运行状况方面仍然很糟糕。您所拥有的唯一元素是SMART状态行，该行声明＆＃34; Verified＆＃34;或＆＃34;失败，＆＃34;没有真实的渐变，也没有以任何方式呈现给用户的数据。

note_en:
Complaints about the potential lifespan of  Apple Silicon Mac storage are causing concern that the current crop of M1 Macs could stop working within a few years — and have been stoked by unanswered questions, unreliable data, and a silent Apple.
  Since the middle of February, social media postings by some M1 Mac users looking into the health of their computer's storage have raised potential issues that they see with their new devices. It is believed that the onboard storage may reach a point where it becomes unusable at a rate some users believe is too fast to be practical.
 The problem stems from a limitation that solid state drives have, in that each cell can only be written to a finite number of times before it fails. This is caused by the repeated erasure of a cell before new data is written, which can wear down the cells to a point where they don't hold a charge.
 Such a drive's potential lifespan can be referred to as Terabytes Written (TBW), referring to how much data can be written to a drive that is covered by the manufacturer before there's a chance it becomes unusable. In the vast majority of cases, the TBW limit of a drive will be multiple times larger than the capacity of the drive itself, and typically equates to years or even a decade of usage for most users.
 In social media posts  collated by  iMore on Tuesday, some users are discovering that their usage of the amount of storage is higher than they expected. Using a tool called  Smart Monitoring Tools in Terminal, the drive's SMART data brings up a "Percentage Used" score, which refers to how much of the TBW a drive has achieved in its lifespan, among other data.
 One user complains that their M1 Mac's SSD is at 1% usage at over 2 months of age. Another's M1  13-inch MacBook Pro with 16GB of memory and 2TB of storage reads as having  used 3% in two months. In some severe cases, the usage percentage is allegedly above 10%.
 Developer Hector Martin  suggested that the 2TB MacBook Pro's 3% results would proportionately equate to around 30% of a 256GB model's usage. "If this is accurate some of these machines aren't going to last half a year to 100%," they reckoned.
 Later, after determining available data suggests endurance ratings aren't  proportionate to drive size, it was suggested the "current known worst case" for a 256GB drive would have it reach 100% in about two years.
 As to why the high disk usage has occurred, a likely culprit could be high swap file usage. This would make the drive usage a bigger problem for Macs with 8GB of memory, especially since many of the published results from the tool are from Macs with 16GB of RAM.
  There are a few problems with the way the story has unfolded. For a start, the lack of decent SMART reporting in macOS itself led users to install tools like the aforementioned Smart Monitoring Tools.
 It is likely that if there were better SMART reporting tools available, rather one that required installation and running it from the terminal, there would be a better picture of the current situation.
 However, the results from the tool may not necessarily be accurate, due to consistent problems with SMART reporting in general. It also isn't clear if the system on a chip and combined caching on the M1 is causing the SMART reporting tools to display incorrect data.
  For example, published results of the tool repeatedly show users as having relatively low counts of "Power On Hours," typically in the low-hundreds and lower. For Macs running for two months, this seems to be a little low compared to what would be expected.
 In  AppleInsider testing with its writers using M1 Macs since the issue first  cropped up on February 13, the power on hours statistic showed 300 hours for one, despite being on for 23 hours a day since its initial purchase on the first day of availability. Another writer has owned their  Mac mini for two months, and has racked up a mere 70 hours on that figure — about nine work days.
 This may indicate that the tool isn't necessarily receiving the correct data in its queries. We've been trying to get some concrete answers, and more data on the issue since February 15.
 Without delving too much into the differences between  different types of flash storage, Apple uses Toshiba TLC NAND flash for its drives in the M1 Macs to date. These are rated at about one complete drive-write per day.
 The cells in an SSD are durable for around 3,000 read and write cycles in Triple Level Cell-based flash memory chips, though more typically around 10,000 cycles for mid-range Multi Level Cell-based chips. Even so, that still equates to over eight years of usage based on the one complete drive-write per day rating.
 Of course, for users who won't need to throw massive amounts of data at their SSD, it's more probable that they will incur less wear, making the drive last longer overall.
 Either way, this is still far above the three to five-year warranties you can expect from an SSD. More expensive professional and enterprise drives can have ten-year warranties, but they are usually accompanied by premium support and use components that are more resilient overall.
 If you take into account that Apple does not use the cheapest flash memory chips on the market in its premium products, this can only reasonably increase the lifespan of the drive itself.
  There is also the fact that the drive won't die if it hits that 100% rating for written data. The TBW is a measure for warranty purposes, with a view to preventing users abusing the storage beyond reasonable usage.
 Even if the storage reaches its rated TBW, it will continue to work until elements of the drive cannot be written to anymore.
 This also doesn't take into account the concept of overprovisioning, when a drive producer includes extra storage capacity in a drive. This isn't to give users more capacity, more to spread the gradual wearing down of the drive over more cells, further prolonging its life.
 We ultimately don't know what quality of chips Apple is using, nor the level of overprovisioning. However, as Apple's first foray into Mac chips, it seems logical that Apple would err on the side of giving a better user experience and be generous on both counts.
 As for the power reporting, it is probable that this is due to Apple replacing the NVMe power management with its own custom system. Apple Fabric, which acts similar to a PCI-E interconnect within M1, may also be interfering with data being fed back into tools.
  "While we're looking into the reports, know that the SMART data being reported to the third-party utility is incorrect, as it pertains to wear on our SSDs" said an  AppleInsider source within Apple corporate not authorized to speak on behalf of the company. The source refused to elaborate any further on the matter when pressed for specifics.
  At this stage, there is no outward indication that Apple is planning to widely discuss the matter. Given the potential for headlines declaring that the "New Mac mini will be dead in a year" or similar, it seems like a PR issue that Apple may want to intercept instead of letting it snowball.
 The affair also raises the question of software tools to determine the health of a Mac. While Apple includes battery health management elements in macOS, the same cannot be said for drive health.
 The nearest thing macOS has to a tool for this purpose is Disk Utility, but while it is useful for managing disks, it remains terrible for determining drive health. The only element you have within is the SMART status line, which either declares "Verified" or "Failing," with no real gradient, or data in any way presented to the user.
 Elements like the Smart Monitoring Tool are difficult for typical Mac users to install and use, but anyone wanting more detail about how their drive is running has to look in that direction. This would be a great chance for Apple to add its own tool, one that correctly reports data about the drive's health given the M1's unique storage implementation.
 Even so, just an official confirmation that it is examining the issue will probably go a long way to ease concerns — even if Apple ends up determining there's no ultimate issue.
 Corrections were made to the mathematics based on older assumptions of TLC cell longevity. While the figures have been reduced downward, the point of the explanation still stands.
